title: The History Of The $1.50 Hot Dog
description: What can a $1.50 hot dog teach you about building a billion-dollar business—or a bulletproof career? In this video, we unpack the Costco story, a retail empire that quietly broke every rule in the book to build radical customer loyalty, industry-defying profits, and one of the strongest internal cultures in business.
transcript:
One of the most beloved retail brands in
America doesn't run ads, doesn't chase
trends, and still sells hot dogs that
lose money on every transaction. This is
the untold story of how an underdog
built one of the most powerful companies
in America by doing the exact opposite
of everyone else. Our story starts with
a forklift driver. Ron Vakas did not go
to Stanford. He didn't get an MBA. He
went to community college and took a job
moving boxes in 1982. He drove forklifts
every day. 40 years later, he is the CEO
of Costco. Last year, he earned $12
million in salary and stock. Ron's
father gave him one simple piece of
advice. Take the worst job at a great
company. The rest is up to you. And
that's exactly what Ron did. For about
35 years, he rotated through every
corner of Costco's business. Warehouse
operations, store level strategy,
regional leadership, new market entry,
starting a new store, real estate, all
of it. By the time the board named him
CEO, there was nothing in the business
that Ron hadn't touched. And he didn't
just understand the business, he had
become the business. What classroom
would ever replicate that kind of
learning? Now, not everybody gets or
even wants the chance to stay in the
same company for 40 years, but Costco
had a culture of building leaders from
within. They were willing to invest. And
Ron showed up every day to earn it. He
believed that titles can be bought, but
leadership has to be earned. So, he kept
building expertise brick by brick until
one day he could lead others and do the
same for them. So, what can we learn
from this? If you're building a career,
treat every job as a training ground. If
you're looking to build depth, go for a
real world experience that helps you
build that expertise and those skills.
And if you're looking for breath, seek
out companies that invest in people and
move you around. When you focus on
mastery over maintenance, it shows
whatever opportunity is in front of you.
Whether it's a forklift or a financial
model, as Ron's dad once said, the rest
is up to you. Costco is famous for its
low prices, but how does it remain
successful with such low prices? Today,
Costco is the third largest retailer in
the world with over 840 stores, which
they, by the way, call warehouses. Their
annual revenue in 2024 was north of $250
billion. Interestingly though, Costco is
partly a subscription business. To shop
at Costco, you need to be a member and
pay an annual fee. That's how they built
their flywheel. So, what's a flywheel?
It was a concept that Jim Collins laid
out in his book, Good to Great. A
flywheel is like a self-reinforcing
system. Once it starts spinning, every
turn makes the next one easier, faster,
and more powerful. It's how great
companies build momentum. Not with big
swings, but with steady pressure in the
same direction. Here's how the flywheel
works for Costco. Membership revenue
funds low prices. Low prices increase
sales volume. More volume means better
supplier deals. Better supplier deals
lead to even lower prices. And that
drives higher membership renewals. That
means more membership profit, which
spins the flywheel right back to the
start. Not bad for a place that sells 30
packs of toilet paper and inflatable
alligators. And those hot dogs, they're
sold at $1.50 even today. Same price
since 1985. Adjusted for inflation, it
should cost three times as much today.
For Costco, though, it is about a
symbolic gesture, a signal that says
we'll fight to keep delivering value to
our members at lower price no matter
what. The hot dog is $1.50 50. Not
because Costco can't do math, but
because some principles are worth more
than profit. This is what Simon SK calls
the infinite game. Playing to keep
playing, not just to win ones and be
done. Costco built its flywheel by
giving more to the customers than it
kept for itself. Turns out our careers
have the same flywheel effect, too.
Think about it. When you consistently
deliver more value than you capture,
like staying late to help a colleague or
sharing credit for wins or teaching
others what you have learned, you build
trust and you build reputation. That
trust and reputation leads you to bigger
opportunities. Bigger opportunities in
turn let you develop more new skills.
New skills make you even more valuable.
And being more valuable means that you
can deliver even more. The compounding
effect of this flywheel just keeps
going. Costco might be the most
counterintuitive retail powerhouse in
the world. Amazon wants to sell you
everything. Costco wins by selling you
less. Simplicity is Costco's core
strategy. That focus lets them go deeper
with the right suppliers. It also
simplifies their supply chain and
operations compared to other retailers.
Fewer products means they need less
physical space and faster inventory
turns. And there is an important
indirect benefit to customers as well.
The shopping experience is not
overwhelming. Shopping at Costco feels
simple, not stressful. It lowers your
cognitive load, but simple doesn't have
to be boring. And Costco understands
that psychology of a shopper very well.
They rotate 20% of items every month. If
you go to a Costco warehouse, sometimes
there's no clear signage on aisles.
Sometimes they just deliberately move
their products around in their
warehouse. And as you walk around
looking for something, there are free
samples everywhere for you to try.
That's the magic formula. Scarcity plus
surprise equals dopamine. This creates a
treasure hunt experience that lights up
the exact same neurological response as
a slot machine. They have engineered
engagement, reliability with just enough
surprise, and customers love it even
more. This is the counterintuitive
genius that's hiding in plain sight.
Every retailer plays to its own
strength. Amazon's amazing technology
and logistics power means that they can
sell and ship millions of products.
Costco, on the other hand, wants to
create a curated product set for its
members in a very unique shopping
experience. Each one builds its own lane
around its core strengths. So, if you're
building your career or business, focus
on things that make you uniquely strong.
That's how you grow fast. Kirkland is
Costco's private label brand and it's a
category of its own. This invisible
brand makes $86 billion every year. In
terms of annual revenue, it's bigger
than Nike. And still, you've never seen
a single ad. Not on TV, not on social
media, not anywhere. They spend zero
dollars on advertising or branding. Why
is that? Well, here's their strategy.
First, many Kirkland products are made
by the premium brands themselves.
Anyway, Kirkland coffee made by
Starbucks, batteries, Duracell, diapers,
Huggies, vodka, Greygus, allegedly.
Second, they're absolutely ruthless
about selecting the right category. If
they can't beat national brands on both
quality and price, they stay out of that
category. That's why Kirkland doesn't
touch categories like electronics or
gadgets. That's the nut they haven't
cracked yet. And third, they don't just
undercut price, they outperform first
and then undercut. The result, a value
equation most brands can't compete with.
There's also another indirect benefit to
customers. Kirkland acts like the price
correction mechanism in that category.
So, when Kirkland enters a category,
national brands can't compete without
dropping prices because Ziploc can't
sell its freezer bags at $18 when
Kirkland freezer bags are $13.99. Of
course, by doing that, Costco's margins
for the same product will be lower than
the brand name. But Costco makes it up
in two ways. by selling in bulk and by
turning shoppers into loyal fee paying
members that will buy the same stuff
over and over again. Sometimes your
output and your work's quality is the
strongest brand you've got. It will
speak for itself. What sets Costco apart
is the design of their stores or lack of
it. Remember how you feel when you walk
into any of the Apple stores? That may
not be how you'll feel walking into
Costco's warehouse. And Costco's look is
frugal by design. Concrete floors,
pallets instead of displays, no music,
no mirrors, no mood lighting. It kind of
echoes Amazon's earliest principles.
Jeff Bezos famously turned a door into a
desk to save money. That same philosophy
lives at Costco because they know what
their customers actually care about.
high quality, low prices, no fluff. This
also means that the company's net
profits are going to be very thin, and
they are 2.8%. That's razor thin. But
Costco passes the value on to customers
first. And interestingly, their
warehouse design has created this
treasure hunt culture that shoppers
appreciate and even enjoy. Costco
doesn't just have loyal customers. Some
would say it has a fullblown cult
following. # Costco has over 11 billion
Tik Tok views. Social media is packed
with Costco dedicated Instagram
accounts, Facebook groups, and Costco
influencers. The Costco food court has
its own cult following. Regional pizza
variations have sparked online wars, and
this strong and loyal following is why
Costco generates $4.8 $8 billion through
membership fees alone. And it has 90%
renewal rate. That's the most sticky and
stable revenue stream anywhere in
retail. This is another example of them
playing the infinite game. Costco
prioritizes the members and their needs,
and the members reward them with trust
and loyalty in the long run. Let's talk
about the risks as well. The world
around Costco is shifting fast. Amazon
and Walmart each hold about 25% of the
US retail market. Costco is a distant
third at 10%. Walmart has Sam's Club
which is exactly like Costco's
membership model and so does Amazon.
Amazon Prime. Amazon has unique
advantages of its own and we all know
what they are. They have massive amount
of data, massive inventory, same day
delivery, digital content, search
history, incredible cloud and AI
capabilities. Costco has none of those
advantages. And in addition, there are
global risks. Tariffs, supply chain
disruptions, US China relationships,
foreign exchange rate fluctuations, oil
price volatility, inflation, and on and
on. The risks will keep on increasing.
And Costco, as you remember, operates on
thin margins. So there is very little
room for error. We're not here to
glorify or condemn anyone or any
company. None of this is financial
advice. I'm not telling you to buy or
sell Costco stock. We're here to learn
from these stories and be inspired and
hopefully inspire others. What are three
final takeaways? First, Costco versus
Amazon versus Walmart. It's really the
battle of relative strengths. They don't
copy each other's playbook. They create
their own set of rules and carve out a
unique brand and value proposition for
their target customers. This applies
directly to all of us as we build our
careers, our businesses, our families,
and our finances. Play to your
strengths. Second, this story reminds me
again about the power of
counterintuitive thinking. Costco breaks
so many conventions in terms of
products, store design, margin
protection, cost, quality, tradeoff. All
of those choices are intentional and
they're interlocked in a way that turns
that flywheel. And finally, Costco's
most inspiring lesson about business and
life is the Ron Bakar story. It reminds
you that no matter where you start,
whether you are a forklift driver or a
front-end designer with or without any
business experience or education, you
can still rise to the top. I invite you
to play the infinite game and grab that
$1.50 hot dog while you're at it. I'll
see you next week. Thank you.
